# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

## Why The Woke Salaryman’s anti-AI art comic misses the point

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman (TWS) published a special comic titled *Why we still stubbornly hand-draw all our art*. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — the comic laid out their principled refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. It was heartfelt, well-intentioned, and, I think, fundamentally wrong.

Not because there aren’t real problems with how AI intersects with art. There are. But TWS’s version of the argument is so broad, so imprecise, and so wrapped up in branding that it actually makes it harder to talk about the cases that really matter.

## The plagiarism claim is doing too much work

The core of TWS’s moral case is that generative AI is trained on “plagiarised imagery.” They state this as fact, build their entire ethical stance on it, and then move on without engaging with any of the complexity underneath.

But plagiarism means something specific. It means taking someone’s work and passing it off as your own. That’s not what happens when a model is trained on a broad dataset of images any more than an art student plagiarises by studying other artists’ work in school. The legal and ethical questions around training data are real and genuinely contested — but “plagiarism” flattens all of that into a word designed to end the conversation rather than start one.

Crucially, TWS never names a victim. No specific artist whose livelihood was destroyed. No particular work that was stolen. The harm stays conveniently abstract.

## Compare this to someone with real skin in the game

Consider photographer Jingna Zhang (zemotion), whose high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of painstaking craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI’s image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel as closely as possible.

That’s not an abstract debate about training data. That’s a specific person using AI as a tool to rip off a specific artist’s specific work.

What makes Zhang’s case so compelling is the asymmetry. She spent years building a distinctive visual language, often on limited budgets — sitting with her team, applying flower petals onto a model one by one, shooting in her apartment with whatever props she could afford. Someone extracted all of that effort in thirty seconds of prompting.

And Zhang isn’t just posting comics about how she feels. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She’s doing the hard, specific work of protecting artists’ rights.

## The person behind the prompt

Here’s what often gets lost in these debates: AI didn’t decide to rip off Jingna Zhang. A human being did. A person looked at her work, decided they wanted it without paying for it, and used AI as the instrument. The intent, the decision, the wrongdoing — all human.

This matters because it completely reframes where the problem lives. If you think the tool is the problem, you get the TWS position: refuse to engage, draw your line, feel good about it. If you think the person behind the prompt is the problem, you need better enforcement, clearer copyright protections, and legal accountability for the humans making those choices.

Blaming the technology is convenient. It lets everyone off the hook. You don’t have to think about enforcement, accountability, or the messy specifics of who did what to whom. You just say “AI bad” and move on.

## The two-sides-of-the-coin problem

Perhaps the most telling moment in the comic is when TWS pivots from their moral stance to acknowledging why others might use AI art. They frown at it, they say, but they also consider: maybe the company doesn’t have the budget, maybe their customers don’t care, maybe competitors are outpacing them.

You can’t have it both ways. If generative AI imagery is built on stolen work, then budget constraints don’t justify using stolen work. You wouldn’t say “I understand why someone shoplifts — maybe they can’t afford things.” If it’s a moral issue, it’s a moral issue regardless of circumstances.

But if those practical considerations are legitimate — if business realities sometimes justify using Gen AI — then your principled stand isn’t really principled. It’s a preference. A luxury you can afford because your brand identity happens to be built around hand-drawn art.

## Performance over conviction

The comic was drawn 99% in pencil on paper — but TWS normally draws digitally. They switched to a more laborious, “authentic” medium specifically for the comic about how authentic and laborious their process is. That’s not walking the talk. That’s costuming up for the occasion.

And this gets at something deeper. TWS is a content brand. People follow them for the takes, the relatability, the clever framing of complex topics. The illustrations serve the message — they’re a vehicle, not the destination. Nobody follows TWS because the drawings are technically remarkable.

Compare that to Zhang, whose entire value proposition is the craft itself. When someone copies her work, they’re stealing the thing that makes her art valuable. When AI hypothetically replicates TWS’s simple character drawings with speech bubbles, what exactly is being taken?

The comic’s emphasis on process — “we go through rounds of sketches, we try different angles” — reads like an attempt to locate artistic credibility in the labor, because the output alone doesn’t carry that weight. “We hand-draw everything” isn’t a principled stand. It’s a brand differentiator in a market increasingly flooded with AI content. It’s smart marketing dressed up as conviction.

## What actually helps artists

The real tragedy of arguments like TWS’s is that they dilute attention from the cases that matter. While content brands post comics about how noble they are for refusing to touch AI, artists like Zhang are fighting specific, winnable battles — in courts, through platform-building, through advocacy that targets the actual mechanisms of harm.

Lumping every use of generative AI into a single moral bucket — from someone turning their beach selfie into an anime portrait to someone deliberately replicating a working artist’s signature style — makes it harder to focus on the genuine abuses. It makes the whole conversation dumber.

The problem was never the technology. It was always the people who misuse it. And drawing lines in the sand, however photogenic, doesn’t change that.

Let me think about this carefully in context of their brand strategy and voice.This is a strong piece, and yes, I think it's worth posting — but let me walk through why, and flag a few things worth considering before you hit publish.

**Why it works for your brand:**

This fits squarely into your Lane 1 (News Commentary) template. You've got a specific, timely hook (TWS comic, March 2026), a contrarian but nuanced take, and you're doing the thing your readers love most — cutting through broad sentiment to find the specific, actionable truth underneath. The Jingna Zhang comparison is excellent. It grounds your argument in exactly the kind of concrete, specific example that defines your voice.

It also lands on your "Say What Others Can't" value proposition. TWS is popular in Singapore. Pushing back on them with a reasoned argument rather than a hot take is the kind of thing Cheryl Seah was talking about when she said you "answer to no one."

**What I'd flag:**

First, the solo counsel return is missing. Your best posts always circle back to "what does this mean for resource-constrained practitioners?" Right now the piece ends on a general point about technology vs. people. There's a natural landing spot: solo counsels and small legal teams are exactly the kind of people who might use AI-generated visuals for pitches, internal decks, or even marketing — not because they want to steal from artists, but because they genuinely can't budget for custom illustration. Your argument actually *serves* your audience, but you haven't made that connection explicit yet.

Second, the tone runs hotter than your usual voice. Your signature move is "neither is wrong." Here, you come close to saying TWS is just wrong. Lines like "smart marketing dressed up as conviction" and "costuming up for the occasion" are sharp — maybe sharper than your usual register. That's not necessarily bad, but it's worth being intentional about. Your readers trust you because you show nuance. A few moments where you acknowledge what TWS gets right (even while disagreeing) would strengthen the piece, not weaken it.

Third, the "shoplifting" analogy in the two-sides section. You're using it to expose TWS's logical inconsistency, but it could read as you comparing AI art use to shoplifting. Worth tightening the framing so it's unmistakably their logic you're stress-testing, not yours.

**Bottom line:** The core argument is sharp, the Zhang comparison is your best material, and this is exactly the kind of piece that earns you the elite engagement you've been building. I'd suggest one revision pass focused on adding the solo counsel angle and softening two or three of the sharpest lines — not to pull punches, but to sound like *you*.

I should frame this in a way that helps them see how their instinct aligns with what they've already been building.You're right to push back. Forcing a "solo counsel angle" onto this piece would feel grafted on. The piece isn't about lawyers using Canva.

But here's what I think you're sitting on, and it's actually bigger than the solo counsel return:

The TWS piece is a case study for something you've been circling around in your legal tech writing. The core argument — that the problem is always the person behind the prompt, not the technology itself — is exactly the argument that matters for the future of legal practice.

Think about it. The legal profession is having its own TWS moment right now. You've got firms and regulators drawing blanket lines: "we don't use AI for drafting," "AI-generated work product must be fully verified," "we're banning ChatGPT." Some of those lines are sensible. Some are the legal equivalent of drawing a pencil comic about how noble you are for not touching the tool.

The real question — your question — is: **what does it mean to be the person behind the prompt when you're a lawyer?**

That's a professional responsibility question, not a technology question. When a lawyer uses AI to draft a contract and doesn't check the output, the failing isn't the AI. It's the lawyer who abdicated judgment. When a lawyer uses AI well — to move faster, to catch things they'd miss, to serve clients they couldn't otherwise afford to help — that's also a human decision.

Your TWS piece already makes this argument. You just haven't named the destination yet. The "person behind the prompt" framing is your bridge. It connects:

- TWS refusing to engage with specifics → lawyers refusing to engage with AI specifics
- Zhang fighting real, targeted battles → practitioners building real accountability frameworks
- "Lines in the sand" as performance → blanket AI policies as institutional performance
- The moral compass living in the human, not the tool → professional judgment as the thing that actually matters

So rather than shoehorning a "for solo counsels" paragraph at the end, what if the piece ends by opening the door? Something that says: this isn't just about art. Every profession facing AI has to decide whether to draw lines in the sand or do the harder work of figuring out what responsible use actually looks like. And for some of us, that question is about to get very personal.

You don't have to say "lawyer" explicitly. Your readers will know exactly what you mean.

Does that feel closer to what you're reaching for?

input.file_text:
# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

## Why refusing AI misses the point — and what's actually worth defending

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a special comic titled *Why we still stubbornly hand-draw all our art*. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their principled refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. It was heartfelt, well-intentioned, and I think it gets the important thing wrong.

Not because there aren't real problems with how AI intersects with art. There are. But TWS's version of the argument is so broad that it actually makes it harder to talk about the cases that really matter.

## The external frame

The core of TWS's moral case is that generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." They state this as fact, build their entire ethical stance on it, and move on.

But plagiarism means something specific. It means taking someone's work and passing it off as your own. That's not what happens when a model is trained on a broad dataset of images any more than an art student plagiarises by studying other artists' work. The legal and ethical questions around training data are genuinely contested — but "plagiarism" flattens all of that into a word designed to end the conversation rather than start one.

And TWS never names a victim. No specific artist whose livelihood was destroyed. No particular work that was stolen. The harm stays conveniently abstract.

This is what I'd call the external frame. The problem is out there. The villains are out there. And the solution is clean and comfortable: refuse to touch the tool.

## Someone with real skin in the game

Consider photographer Jingna Zhang, whose high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of painstaking craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel as closely as possible.

That's not an abstract debate about training data. That's a specific person using AI as a tool to rip off a specific artist's specific work.

Zhang isn't posting comics about how she feels. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She's doing the hard, specific work of protecting artists' rights.

But here's what I keep coming back to: AI didn't decide to rip off Jingna Zhang. A human being did. A person looked at her work, decided they wanted it without paying for it, and used AI as the instrument. The intent, the decision, the wrongdoing — all human.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

There's a detail in Zhang's case that I can't stop thinking about.

The difference between a portrait where the eyes are closed and one where they're open isn't technical. It's a *decision*. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That choice — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

When someone prompts AI to reproduce her work, they're not stealing pixels. They're stealing the decision. They're collapsing years of accumulated judgment into "make it look like this."

And that's where the real anger lives. Not in the image being copied. In the erasure of the judgment behind it. Someone looked at the *output* of Zhang's decisions and treated it as if the decisions didn't exist. As if the only thing that mattered was the result, not the person who made the choices that produced it.

## The tension nobody talks about

TWS's argument is a luxury. It lets you draw a clean line and stand on the right side of it. But most of us don't get that clean exit.

Here's the tension TWS never touches: "I believe there are genuine ethical concerns about this technology AND I need to use it AND nobody has given me a clear framework for how to do both."

That's not an art problem. That's every profession right now.

TWS even gestures at this — they acknowledge that some businesses can't afford custom illustration, that competitors are outpacing companies that refuse AI. But they never sit with the contradiction. If AI-generated imagery is built on stolen work, budget constraints don't justify using it. You wouldn't say "I understand why someone takes things they can't afford." If it's a moral issue, it's a moral issue regardless of circumstances.

But if those practical considerations are legitimate — if business realities sometimes justify using generative AI — then your principled stand isn't really principled. It's a preference you can afford because your brand identity happens to be built around hand-drawn art.

Most professionals don't get to opt out. They have to use these tools AND hold onto their judgment about how.

## What are we actually fighting for

If we push the idea that there is always a person behind the prompt — and I believe we should — then we have to ask what that's fighting *against*.

It's not fighting against AI. It's fighting against the quiet logic creeping into every industry: **the output is all that matters.**

Not "AI will replace you." Something subtler. It's: "the client can't tell the difference." "The contract looks fine." "The portrait looks beautiful." If the result is indistinguishable, why pay for the judgment?

Once you accept that framing, you've already lost. Because you've agreed that Zhang's years of learning why eyes closed works better — that's overhead. A lawyer's instinct that this particular clause will blow up in three years — overhead. A doctor's gut feeling about a diagnosis that technically fits the symptoms — overhead. Anything that doesn't show up in the deliverable is overhead.

That's what Zhang is actually angry about. Not that someone copied her image. That someone proved you could *skip her* and get something that looks close enough. The implicit argument: your judgment was never the product. The picture was.

## The decisions behind the output

This is where it gets personal for anyone who takes their craft seriously.

A contract isn't words on a page. It's a sequence of decisions — why this clause and not that one, why this risk allocation, why this term is scoped this way. A photograph isn't pixels. It's eyes closed instead of open. A medical diagnosis isn't the conclusion. It's the reasoning that got there.

AI can produce outputs that look like all of these things. And the pressure — from markets, from clients, from employers — will increasingly be: the output looks fine, so the process is optional.

If we collectively shrug and say "close enough," we're not just devaluing Zhang's craft or any individual professional's expertise. We're saying that the human capacity to make considered decisions isn't worth preserving unless it's commercially distinguishable from a machine.

That's bleak. And it's worth fighting.

## Not lines in the sand

But not with lines in the sand. Not with blanket refusals that let us feel righteous without engaging with specifics. Not with pencil-drawn comics about how noble we are for not touching the tool.

The fight that matters is for the idea that decisions have value — even when nobody can see them in the output. That the person behind the prompt carries responsibility not just for misuse, but for good use. That professional judgment isn't overhead to be optimised away, but the thing that makes the output trustworthy in the first place.

Every profession facing AI has to decide: do we draw lines and feel good about it, or do we do the harder work of figuring out what it means to own our decisions in a world where the output can be generated without us?

I know which question I'm sitting with.

input.file_text:
# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

Many law firms have their own version of the line in the sand. "We don't use generative AI." "All AI-assisted work must be fully human-reviewed." These sound like principled positions. Often they're closer to branding — a way to signal trustworthiness without engaging with the harder questions underneath.

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a comic that crystallises this pattern. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. The moral case rested on a single claim: generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." Stated as fact, never interrogated, and broad enough to justify a clean exit from the entire conversation.

That's the appeal of lines in the sand. They let you stand on the right side without getting into specifics.

## Getting specific

Photographer Jingna Zhang forces you into specifics.

Zhang's high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel.

AI didn't decide to do this. A person looked at her work, decided they wanted it without paying for it, and used AI as the instrument. The intent, the decision, the wrongdoing — all human.

Zhang didn't draw a line. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She engaged with the specific mechanisms of harm rather than retreating to abstraction.

But there's a detail in her case that I keep returning to, because I think it locates the real issue better than any debate about training data.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

The difference between a portrait where the eyes are closed and one where they're open isn't technical. It's a decision. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

When someone prompts AI to reproduce her work, they're not stealing pixels. They're erasing the decision. They're collapsing years of accumulated craft into "make it look like this." The output might be indistinguishable. But the act of deciding is gone.

That's where the real anger lives. Not in the copy. In the implication that the decisions never mattered — that the photograph was always just the photograph, and the human who made the choices that produced it was incidental.

## The quiet logic

This erasure isn't limited to art. There's a logic creeping into every profession: if the output looks right, the process is optional.

Not "AI will replace you." Something more subtle. "The client can't tell the difference." "The contract looks fine." "The portrait looks beautiful." If the result is indistinguishable, the reasoning behind it is overhead.

A contract isn't words on a page. It's a sequence of decisions — this clause and not that one, this risk allocation, this term scoped this way. A medical diagnosis isn't the conclusion. It's the clinical reasoning that arrived there. A photograph isn't the image. It's eyes closed instead of open.

AI can produce outputs that look like all of these things. And the pressure — from markets, from clients, from the sheer convenience of it — is to accept the output and skip the process.

## The tension we live with

This is where TWS's argument falls apart as a guide for anyone who actually has to work.

Their stance is entirely outward-facing. The problem is out there — in the training data, in the companies that built the models, in the people who use them. The solution: refuse. Draw your line.

But most professionals don't get that exit. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it. Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

Law firms that declare "we don't use AI" are performing the same move as TWS drawing in pencil for a comic about drawing in pencil. It looks like conviction. It functions as avoidance.

## What AI actually reveals

Here's what I think gets missed in almost every version of this debate.

AI doesn't threaten human judgment. It reveals whether you were exercising any.

Before AI, you could coast. Draft by precedent. Copy the last version. Reuse the template. Nobody could easily distinguish between a considered decision and a reflexive one, because the output looked the same either way.

Now a machine can produce that same output. Which means the only thing left that's distinctly yours is the act of deciding. Not the product of the decision. The decision itself. The intentionality.

That's not a loss. It's a clarification.

Choosing eyes closed over eyes open. Choosing this clause over that one. Choosing to question a diagnosis that technically fits. These aren't valuable because they produce better outputs — sometimes they don't, sometimes you can't tell. They're what it means to practise. They're what it means to be a professional human being doing considered work. They always were. AI just stripped away the ability to pretend otherwise.

## Not lines, but decisions

So the question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

The harder path is what Zhang chose. Engage with the specifics. Name the harm. Build the tools. Fight the fights that matter. And keep making the decisions that are yours to make, knowing that the act of making them is the point — not because it produces something a machine can't, but because that's what it means to do this work as a human being.

I know which path I'm trying to walk.

Many law firms have their own version of the line in the sand. "We don't use generative AI." "All AI-assisted work must be fully human-reviewed." These sound like principled positions. Often they're closer to branding — a way to signal trustworthiness without engaging with the harder questions underneath.

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a comic that crystallises this pattern. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. The moral case rested on a single claim: generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." Stated as fact, never interrogated, and broad enough to justify a clean exit from the entire conversation.

That's the appeal of lines in the sand. They let you stand on the right side without getting into specifics.

## Getting specific

Photographer Jingna Zhang forces you into specifics.

Zhang's high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel.

AI didn't decide to do this. A person looked at her work, decided they wanted it without paying for it, and used AI as the instrument. The intent, the decision, the wrongdoing — all human.

Zhang didn't draw a line. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She engaged with the specific mechanisms of harm rather than retreating to abstraction.

But there's a detail in her case that I keep returning to, because I think it locates the real issue better than any debate about training data.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

The difference between a portrait where the eyes are closed and one where they're open isn't technical. It's a decision. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

When someone prompts AI to reproduce her work, they're not stealing pixels. They're erasing the decision. They're collapsing years of accumulated craft into "make it look like this." The output might be indistinguishable. But the act of deciding is gone.

That's where the real anger lives. Not in the copy. In the implication that the decisions never mattered — that the photograph was always just the photograph, and the human who made the choices that produced it was incidental.

## The quiet logic

This erasure isn't limited to art. There's a logic creeping into every profession: if the output looks right, the process is optional.

Not "AI will replace you." Something more subtle. "The client can't tell the difference." "The contract looks fine." "The portrait looks beautiful." If the result is indistinguishable, the reasoning behind it is overhead.

A contract isn't words on a page. It's a sequence of decisions — this clause and not that one, this risk allocation, this term scoped this way. A medical diagnosis isn't the conclusion. It's the clinical reasoning that arrived there. A photograph isn't the image. It's eyes closed instead of open.

AI can produce outputs that look like all of these things. And the pressure — from markets, from clients, from the sheer convenience of it — is to accept the output and skip the process.

## The tension we live with

This is where TWS's argument falls apart as a guide for anyone who actually has to work.

Their stance is entirely outward-facing. The problem is out there — in the training data, in the companies that built the models, in the people who use them. The solution: refuse. Draw your line.

But most professionals don't get that exit. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it. Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

Law firms that declare "we don't use AI" are performing the same move as TWS drawing in pencil for a comic about drawing in pencil. It looks like conviction. It functions as avoidance.

## What AI actually reveals

Here's what I think gets missed in almost every version of this debate.

AI doesn't threaten human judgment. It reveals whether you were exercising any.

Before AI, you could coast. Draft by precedent. Copy the last version. Reuse the template. Nobody could easily distinguish between a considered decision and a reflexive one, because the output looked the same either way.

Now a machine can produce that same output. Which means the only thing left that's distinctly yours is the act of deciding. Not the product of the decision. The decision itself. The intentionality.

That's not a loss. It's a clarification.

Choosing eyes closed over eyes open. Choosing this clause over that one. Choosing to question a diagnosis that technically fits. These aren't things we defend because they produce better outputs — sometimes they don't, sometimes you can't tell. They're what it means to practise. They're what it means to be a human being doing considered work. They always were. AI just stripped away the ability to pretend otherwise.

## Not lines, but decisions

So the question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

The harder path is what Zhang chose. Engage with the specifics. Name the harm. Build the tools. Fight the fights that matter. And keep making the decisions that are yours to make — not because it produces something a machine can't, but because that's what it means to do this work.

I know which path I'm trying to walk.

chat_messages.text:
The user is pushing me to think deeper about intentionality as a concept, not just as a proxy for liability/responsibility, but as something more fundamental about what it means to be human in professional work. They're making an important distinction: intentionality isn't about expertise or experience. A young lawyer can be just as intentional as a senior one. 

Let me think about how this plays out in the examples we've been working with.

This is a really interesting philosophical point. Let me think through it carefully.

Intentionality as the user is framing it isn't about making the RIGHT decision. It's about making A decision. It's the act of engaging, of choosing, of being present in the work. 

That's the democratic part—it doesn't hinge on accumulated expertise. But looking back at Zhang's case, the person who used AI to replicate her work was technically making a choice too. So intentionality itself can't be the full answer. The real distinction seems to be about where intentionality is directed: the prompter was intentional about the final output—"make it look like Zhang"—but delegated every actual decision to the machine. Zhang's intentionality was embedded in the process itself, in the choices made moment by moment. It's not just about wanting something; it's about being present in how you get there.

The young lawyer example clarifies this further. A first-year associate might make a worse call than a senior partner, but if they're genuinely thinking through the clause, weighing the implications, they're being intentional in a way that matters. Experience sharpens judgment, but intentionality is the act of deciding itself. That's why TWS's blanket "no AI" stance is almost anti-intentional—they've made one sweeping decision that lets them avoid all the harder, specific choices about where AI actually fits. And this connects to something deeper: the real threat isn't just privileging output over process, it's that we stop being the ones making decisions at all.This is an important distinction and I think it sharpens the piece considerably.

If intentionality is about experience or expertise, then the argument becomes elitist. Only seasoned practitioners have judgment worth preserving. AI threatens the masters. That's the Zhang reading if you're not careful — years of accumulated craft, irreplaceable expertise, etc.

But that's not what you're saying. You're saying intentionality is the act of deciding. Not deciding *well*. Deciding at all.

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this indemnity cap because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. A senior lawyer might choose differently. But they're *in the work*. They're the human behind the decision. Compare that to a senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking. Thirty years of experience, zero intentionality.

That's a radical reframe. It means AI doesn't threaten expertise. It threatens *engagement*.

Now look at how this plays in the examples:

**Zhang's plagiarist** wasn't lacking experience. They were intentional about the *output* — "make it look like this." But they delegated every decision within the work to the machine. They intended to arrive at a destination without walking any of the path. That's the distinction: intending a result versus intending each choice along the way.

**Zhang** chose eyes closed. Maybe a younger photographer would have chosen eyes open. Both are intentional. The quality of the decision varies with experience. The act of deciding doesn't.

**TWS** is actually the most interesting case under this framing. Their blanket refusal looks like a strong decision. But it's actually one decision that replaces hundreds of smaller ones. "We don't use AI" means you never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What would responsible use look like in this specific case? What am I choosing and why? It's a single act of intentionality that exempts you from all the ongoing ones.

**Law firms** doing the same — "no AI policy" — are doing the same thing. One big decision to avoid a thousand small ones.

So the piece might be arguing: intentionality isn't a trait of experienced professionals. It's a practice. It's what makes you a practitioner at all, regardless of seniority. And the real danger of AI isn't that it replaces expert judgment — it's that it gives everyone, junior and senior alike, a way to stop engaging with the decisions entirely.

Does that track? If so, I think the "What AI actually reveals" section needs reworking — it currently leans too hard on the "coasting by precedent" framing, which implies the problem is experienced people being lazy. The problem is bigger: it's anyone, at any level, ceding the act of deciding.

input.file_text:
# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

Many law firms have their own version of the line in the sand. "We don't use generative AI." "All AI-assisted work must be fully human-reviewed." These sound like principled positions. Often they're closer to branding — a way to signal trustworthiness without engaging with the harder questions underneath.

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a comic that crystallises this pattern. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. The moral case rested on a single claim: generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." Stated as fact, never interrogated, and broad enough to justify a clean exit from the entire conversation.

That's the appeal of lines in the sand. They let you stand on the right side without getting into specifics. But I think the specifics reveal something these broad positions keep missing — something about what it actually means to be the human in the equation.

## Getting specific

Photographer Jingna Zhang forces you into specifics.

Zhang's high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel.

Zhang didn't draw a line. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She engaged with the specific mechanisms of harm rather than retreating to abstraction.

But there's a detail in her case that I keep returning to, because I think it locates something that the training data debate never reaches.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

The difference between a portrait where the eyes are closed and one where they're open isn't technical. It's a decision. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

AI doesn't make this choice. It resolves a statistical pattern. When an image model produces a portrait with eyes closed, nothing chose. There's no moment of "this, not that." There's a probability distribution that settled on an output. The decision isn't absent because the AI is bad at deciding. It's absent because deciding isn't something AI does.

When someone prompted AI to reproduce Zhang's work, they weren't just stealing pixels. They were collapsing her act of choosing into "make it look like this." And the AI obliged — not because it understood what it was reproducing, but because understanding isn't part of the process.

That's where the real anger lives. Not in the copy. In the revelation that the output can exist without anyone having decided anything about it.

## The training data is inert

This reframes something TWS never engages with.

Their moral case locates the problem in the training data — the millions of images that went *into* the model. But the training data doesn't do anything. It sits in the model the way paint sits in a tube. Paint doesn't plagiarise. A person picks up the brush.

The entire moral weight of AI lives in the human act of bringing something out. Zhang's plagiarist intended a specific result. They decided to extract something that looked like her work. The AI didn't decide to rip her off. It can't decide anything.

This isn't a technicality. It's the whole story. If AI cannot practise intentionality — and it can't — then the person behind the prompt isn't just part of the moral equation. They *are* the moral equation. The only place where responsibility exists is the only place where intention exists: the human.

Which means blanket positions about the technology — TWS's refusal, law firms' AI bans — are aimed at the wrong target. The technology is inert. The decisions are, and always were, human.

## The tension we actually live with

But this doesn't let anyone off the hook. If anything, it makes things harder.

If the moral weight lives entirely with the human, then you can't outsource your conscience to a policy. "We don't use AI" is one decision that replaces a thousand smaller ones. You never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What am I choosing and why? What decisions need to remain mine?

Most professionals don't get the clean exit TWS takes. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it. Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

But maybe the framework is simpler than we think. If AI can't decide, then the question is always: **am I still deciding?**

## What AI actually reveals

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this indemnity cap because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. A senior lawyer might choose differently. But they're *in the work*. They're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do.

Compare that to a senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking. Thirty years of experience. Zero intentionality. They weren't practising law. They were producing output. AI just makes it obvious that there was no difference between what they were doing and what a machine does.

This is true across every field. A doctor who follows a diagnostic algorithm without clinical reasoning. A designer who applies the same layout to every brief. A photographer who shoots the same composition by reflex. Before AI, these habits were invisible — the output looked the same as considered work. Now a machine can match that output, and the distinction is suddenly visible: were you deciding, or were you just producing?

AI doesn't threaten intentionality. It reveals its absence. It strips away the ability to pretend that going through the motions is the same as engaging with the work.

That's not a threat. It's a clarification.

## Not lines, but decisions

So the question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails. Whether we're the human behind the work or just the human near it.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

The harder path is what Zhang chose. Engage with the specifics. Name the harm. Build the tools. Fight the fights that matter. And keep deciding — not because the decisions produce something a machine can't, but because the act of deciding is what makes you a practitioner at all.

A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it.

I know which side of that distinction I'm trying to stay on.

Zhang's high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel.

Zhang didn't draw a line. She fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She engaged with the specific mechanisms of harm rather than retreating to abstraction.

But there's a detail in her case that I keep returning to, because I think it locates something that the training data debate never reaches.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

The difference between a portrait where the eyes are closed and one where they're open isn't technical. It's a decision. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

AI doesn't make this choice. It resolves a statistical pattern. When an image model produces a portrait with eyes closed, nothing chose. There's no moment of "this, not that." There's a probability distribution that settled on an output. The decision isn't absent because the AI is bad at deciding. It's absent because deciding isn't something AI does.

When someone prompted AI to reproduce Zhang's work, they weren't just stealing pixels. They were collapsing her act of choosing into "make it look like this." And the AI obliged — not because it understood what it was reproducing, but because understanding isn't part of the process.

That's where the real anger lives. Not in the copy. In the revelation that the output can exist without anyone having decided anything about it.

## The training data is inert

This reframes something TWS never engages with.

Their moral case locates the problem in the training data — the millions of images that went *into* the model. But the training data doesn't do anything. It sits in the model the way paint sits in a tube. Paint doesn't plagiarise. A person picks up the brush.

The entire moral weight of AI lives in the human act of bringing something out. Zhang's plagiarist intended a specific result. They decided to extract something that looked like her work. The AI didn't decide to rip her off. It can't decide anything.

This isn't a technicality. It's the whole story. If AI cannot practise intentionality — and it can't — then the person behind the prompt isn't just part of the moral equation. They *are* the moral equation. The only place where responsibility exists is the only place where intention exists: the human.

Which means blanket positions about the technology — TWS's refusal, law firms' AI bans — are aimed at the wrong target. The technology is inert. The decisions are, and always were, human.

## The tension we actually live with

But this doesn't let anyone off the hook. If anything, it makes things harder.

If the moral weight lives entirely with the human, then you can't outsource your conscience to a policy. "We don't use AI" is one decision that replaces a thousand smaller ones. You never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What am I choosing and why? What decisions need to remain mine?

Most professionals don't get the clean exit TWS takes. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it. Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

But maybe the framework is simpler than we think. If AI can't decide, then the question is always: **am I still deciding?**

## What AI actually reveals

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this indemnity cap because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. A senior lawyer might choose differently. But they're *in the work*. They're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do.

Compare that to a senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking. Thirty years of experience. Zero intentionality. They weren't practising law. They were producing output. AI just makes it obvious that there was no difference between what they were doing and what a machine does.

This is true across every field. A doctor who follows a diagnostic algorithm without clinical reasoning. A designer who applies the same layout to every brief. A photographer who shoots the same composition by reflex. Before AI, these habits were invisible — the output looked the same as considered work. Now a machine can match that output, and the distinction is suddenly visible: were you deciding, or were you just producing?

AI doesn't threaten intentionality. It reveals its absence. It strips away the ability to pretend that going through the motions is the same as engaging with the work.

That's not a threat. It's a clarification.

## Not lines, but decisions

So the question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails. Whether we're the human behind the work or just the human near it.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

The harder path is what Zhang chose. Engage with the specifics. Name the harm. Build the tools. Fight the fights that matter. And keep deciding — not because the decisions produce something a machine can't, but because the act of deciding is what makes you a practitioner at all.

A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it.

I know which side of that distinction I'm trying to stay on.

# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

Many law firms have their own version of the line in the sand. "We don't use generative AI." "All AI-assisted work must be fully human-reviewed." These sound like principled positions. Often they're closer to branding — a way to signal trustworthiness without engaging with the harder questions underneath.

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a comic that crystallises this pattern. Drawn almost entirely in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their refusal to use generative AI for any of their artwork. The moral case rested on a single claim: generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." Stated as fact, never interrogated, and broad enough to justify a clean exit from the entire conversation.

That's the appeal of lines in the sand. They let you stand on the right side without getting into specifics. But I think the specifics reveal something these broad positions keep missing — something about what it actually means to be the human in the equation.

## Getting specific

Photographer Jingna Zhang forces you into specifics.

Zhang's high-end fantasy portrait work represents years of craft development. In 2025, someone took one of her photographs and ran it through OpenAI's image model, with prompts explicitly instructing the AI to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel.

Eyes open conveys confrontation, connection, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — knowing why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

AI doesn't make this choice. It resolves a statistical pattern. When an image model produces a portrait with eyes closed, nothing chose. There's no moment of "this, not that." There's a probability distribution that settled on an output. The decision isn't absent because the AI is bad at deciding. It's absent because deciding isn't something AI does.

When someone prompted AI to reproduce Zhang's work, they weren't just stealing pixels. They were collapsing her act of choosing into "make it look like this." And the AI obliged — not because it understood what it was reproducing, but because understanding isn't part of the process.

That's where the real anger lives. Not in the copy. In the revelation that the output can exist without anyone having decided anything about it.

## The training data is inert

This reframes something TWS never engages with.

Their moral case locates the problem in the training data — the millions of images that went *into* the model. But the training data doesn't do anything. It sits in the model the way paint sits in a tube. Paint doesn't plagiarise. A person picks up the brush.

The entire moral weight of AI lives in the human act of bringing something out. Zhang's plagiarist intended a specific result. They decided to extract something that looked like her work. The AI didn't decide to rip her off. It can't decide anything.

This isn't a technicality. It's the whole story. If AI cannot practise intentionality — and it can't — then the person behind the prompt isn't just part of the moral equation. They *are* the moral equation. The only place where responsibility exists is the only place where intention exists: the human.

Which means blanket positions about the technology — TWS's refusal, law firms' AI bans — are aimed at the wrong target. The technology is inert. The decisions are, and always were, human.

## The tension we actually live with

But this doesn't let anyone off the hook. If anything, it makes things harder.

If the moral weight lives entirely with the human, then you can't outsource your conscience to a policy. "We don't use AI" is one decision that replaces a thousand smaller ones. You never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What am I choosing and why? What decisions need to remain mine?

Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

But maybe the framework is simpler than we think. If AI can't decide, then the question is always: **am I still deciding?**

## What AI actually reveals

Here's the conventional wisdom: AI replaces junior lawyers first, because juniors do routine work and AI can do routine work. The implication — sometimes said quietly, sometimes not — is that seniority is the moat. Experience protects you. Put in your years and you'll be fine.

This gets the problem exactly backwards.

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this indemnity cap because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. A senior lawyer might choose differently. But they're *in the work*. They're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do.

Compare that to a senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking. Thirty years of experience. Zero intentionality. They weren't practising law. They were producing output. AI just makes it obvious that there was never a difference between what they were doing and what a machine does.

AI doesn't threaten intentionality. It reveals its absence. It strips away the ability to pretend that going through the motions is the same as engaging with the work.

Seniority was never the moat. Deciding was.

## What a junior lawyer actually does

So if you're starting your career in a profession that's being reshaped by AI, how do you not get replaced?

Not by being faster. Not by knowing more. Not by waiting for your firm to figure out its AI policy. You start deciding.

A template lands on your desk. You can fill it in, or you can ask: what decisions are embedded in this? Who drafted it? What were they protecting against? Would I make the same choices for *this* client, *this* deal? A template is someone else's decisions fossilised. You either interrogate those decisions or you're a delivery mechanism. AI is a very efficient delivery mechanism. You're not going to win that race.

An AI produces a first draft. You can proofread it, or you can ask: if I had written this from scratch, would I have made the same choices? Where would I differ? Can I articulate why? If you can't say why you'd do it differently — or why you'd do it the same way — you haven't reviewed the draft. You've approved output. That's not practice. That's rubber-stamping.

Nobody needs to give you permission to be intentional. Nobody can stop you from being the one who decides. That's how you save yourself: not by outrunning the machine, but by being the thing the machine categorically isn't.

The question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails. Whether we're the human behind the work or just the human near it.

A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it.

I know which side of that distinction I'm trying to stay on.

This isn't a technicality. It's the whole story. If AI cannot practise intentionality — and it can't — then the person behind the prompt isn't just part of the moral equation. They *are* the moral equation. The only place where responsibility exists is the only place where intention exists: the human.

Which means blanket positions about the technology — TWS's refusal, law firms' AI bans — are aimed at the wrong target. The technology is inert. The decisions are, and always were, human.

## The tension we actually live with

But this doesn't let anyone off the hook. If anything, it makes things harder.

If the moral weight lives entirely with the human, then you can't outsource your conscience to a policy. "We don't use AI" is one decision that replaces a thousand smaller ones. You never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What am I choosing and why? What decisions need to remain mine?

Most professionals don't get the clean exit TWS takes. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it. Not by bad actors — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. And nobody has given me a framework for how to hold both of those things at the same time.

But maybe the framework is simpler than we think. If AI can't decide, then the question is always: **am I still deciding?**

## What AI actually reveals

Here's the conventional wisdom: AI replaces junior lawyers first, because juniors do routine work and AI can do routine work. The implication — sometimes said quietly, sometimes not — is that seniority is the moat. Experience protects you. Put in your years and you'll be fine.

This gets the problem exactly backwards.

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this indemnity cap because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. A senior lawyer might choose differently. But they're *in the work*. They're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do.

AI doesn't threaten intentionality. It reveals its absence. It strips away the ability to pretend that going through the motions is the same as engaging with the work.

Seniority was never the moat. Deciding was.

## What a junior lawyer actually does

So if you're starting your career in a profession that's being reshaped by AI, how do you not get replaced?

Not by being faster. Not by knowing more. Not by waiting for your firm to figure out its AI policy. You start deciding.

A template lands on your desk. You can fill it in, or you can ask: what decisions are embedded in this? Who drafted it? What were they protecting against? Would I make the same choices for *this* client, *this* deal? A template is someone else's decisions fossilised. You either interrogate those decisions or you're a delivery mechanism. AI is a very efficient delivery mechanism. You're not going to win that race.

An AI produces a first draft. You can proofread it, or you can ask: if I had written this from scratch, would I have made the same choices? Where would I differ? Can I articulate why? If you can't say why you'd do it differently — or why you'd do it the same way — you haven't reviewed the draft. You've approved output. That's not practice. That's rubber-stamping.

AI surfaces a research summary. You can accept it, or you can ask: what did it leave out? What framing did it choose? What's the question I didn't think to ask?

The move isn't refusing AI. It's refusing to let AI be the one that decides. Every task, no matter how routine it looks, has decisions inside it. The junior lawyer who finds them and makes them is practising law. The one who doesn't — regardless of how many years they have — is producing documents.

Nobody needs to give you permission to be intentional. Nobody can stop you from being the one who decides. That's how you save yourself: not by outrunning the machine, but by being the thing the machine categorically isn't.

## Not lines, but decisions

The question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has already been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Not whether we review the output. Not whether we slap a "human-verified" label on it. Whether we engage in the act of choosing — with all the uncertainty, all the accumulated judgment, all the messy specificity that entails. Whether we're the human behind the work or just the human near it.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

The harder path is what Zhang chose. Engage with the specifics. Name the harm. Build the tools. Fight the fights that matter. And keep deciding — not because the decisions produce something a machine can't, but because deciding is what makes you a practitioner at all.

A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it.

I know which side of that distinction I'm trying to stay on.

input.file_text:
# The Problem with Drawing Lines in the Sand

Many law firms have their own version of the line in the sand. "We don't use generative AI." "All AI-assisted work must be fully human-reviewed." These sound principled. Often they're branding — a way to signal trustworthiness without engaging with the harder questions underneath.

In March 2026, The Woke Salaryman published a comic that crystallises this pattern. Drawn in pencil on paper — a departure from their usual digital workflow — it laid out their refusal to use generative AI for artwork. The moral case rested on a single claim: generative AI is trained on "plagiarised imagery." Stated as fact, never interrogated, and broad enough to justify a clean exit from the entire conversation.

That's the appeal of lines in the sand. You stand on the right side without getting into specifics.

## Eyes closed, eyes open

Photographer Jingna Zhang forces you into specifics.

In 2025, someone ran one of Zhang's high-end fantasy portraits through OpenAI's image model, prompting it to replicate her composition, colours, tone, and feel. Zhang fought and won a landmark copyright case in Luxembourg. She built Cara, a portfolio platform for artists concerned about AI training. She engaged with specific mechanisms of harm rather than retreating to abstraction.

But there's a detail I keep returning to.

The difference between a portrait with eyes closed and one with eyes open isn't technical. It's a decision. Eyes closed conveys vulnerability, intimacy, interiority. Eyes open conveys confrontation, presence. Zhang chose one over the other. That act of choosing — why this and not that, why this light, why this angle — *is* the art.

AI doesn't make this choice. It resolves a statistical pattern. When an image model produces a portrait with eyes closed, nothing chose. There's no moment of "this, not that." There's a probability distribution that settled on an output. Deciding isn't something AI does badly. It's something AI doesn't do.

## The training data is inert

This reframes something TWS never engages with. Their moral case locates the problem in the training data — the millions of images that went *into* the model. But the training data doesn't do anything. It sits in the model the way paint sits in a tube. Paint doesn't plagiarise. A person picks up the brush.

If AI cannot practise intentionality — and it can't — then the person behind the prompt isn't just part of the moral equation. They *are* the moral equation. The only place where responsibility exists is the only place where intention exists: the human.

## Intentionality is morally ambiguous

But here's what we have to sit with: intentionality isn't a virtue. It's a capacity. And it can be used for harm.

Zhang's plagiarist was intentional. They looked at her work, decided they wanted it without paying for it, and used AI to extract it. That wasn't a passive act. It was a deliberate choice — a human exercising their capacity for decision-making in a way that harmed another human.

The tool didn't make the moral choice. The person did, and they chose badly.

This is what makes intentionality distinctly human rather than inherently good. It carries moral weight precisely because it can go either way. Zhang chose eyes closed for a reason. Her plagiarist chose to steal that choice for a reason. Both are acts of intention. Only humans can perform either one.

Which means blanket positions about the technology — TWS's refusal, law firms' AI bans — are aimed at the wrong target entirely. The technology is inert. The decisions are human. And the decisions can be good or terrible. That's not a flaw in the argument. That's the argument.

## The tension we actually live with

Most professionals don't get the clean exit TWS takes. The tension is internal: I know AI has problems. I'm also being told to use it — by my employer, my clients, my own need to stay competitive. Nobody has given me a clear framework for how to hold both.

"We don't use AI" is one big decision that replaces a thousand smaller ones. You never have to ask: would AI be appropriate here? What am I choosing? What decisions need to remain mine? It looks like conviction. It functions as abdication.

And here's the warning: the people who replace you won't be machines. They'll be the humans who are willing to decide when you aren't. Some will decide well. Some will decide badly — like Zhang's plagiarist. But they'll all be *in the work* while you're standing behind your line in the sand.

## What AI actually reveals

The conventional wisdom says AI replaces junior professionals first, because juniors do routine work and AI does routine work. The implication — sometimes said quietly — is that seniority is the moat.

This gets the problem backwards.

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. But they're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do. A senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking? Thirty years of experience. Zero intentionality. AI just makes it obvious that there was never a difference between what they were doing and what a machine does.

Seniority was never the moat. Deciding was.

## How you save yourself

So if you're in a profession being reshaped by AI, how do you not get replaced?

Not by being faster. Not by knowing more. Not by waiting for someone to figure out the policy. You start deciding.

A template lands on your desk. You can fill it in, or you can ask: what decisions are fossilised in this? Would I make the same choices for this client, this deal? A template you haven't interrogated is someone else's intentions you've adopted without examination.

An AI produces a first draft. You can proofread it, or you can ask: if I had written this, would I have made the same choices? Where would I differ? If you can't say why, you haven't reviewed the draft. You've rubber-stamped output.

Every task, no matter how routine, has decisions inside it. The person who finds them and makes them is practising. The person who doesn't is producing. Nobody needs to give you permission to be intentional.

## Not lines, but decisions

The question facing every profession isn't whether to use AI. That line has been crossed. The question is whether we still decide.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it.

That choice can be wise or harmful, expert or naive, generous or selfish. It's human either way. And it's the only thing in this equation that is.

## How you save yourself

So if you're in a profession being reshaped by AI, how do you not get replaced?

A template lands on your desk. You can fill it in, or you can ask: what decisions are fossilised in this? Would I make the same choices for this client, this deal? A template you haven't interrogated is someone else's intentions you've adopted without examination.

input.old_str:
## What AI actually reveals

The conventional wisdom says AI replaces junior professionals first, because juniors do routine work and AI does routine work. The implication — sometimes said quietly — is that seniority is the moat.

This gets the problem backwards.

A junior lawyer who reads a clause and thinks "I'm choosing this because of X" is being intentional. They might be wrong. But they're doing the thing AI categorically cannot do. A senior lawyer who copies the last template without thinking? Thirty years of experience. Zero intentionality. AI just makes it obvious that there was never a difference between what they were doing and what a machine does.

Seniority was never the moat. Deciding was.
input.new_str:
## What AI actually reveals

There's a comforting story making the rounds: AI handles the low-end work, which frees humans for the high-end work. Juniors are at risk. Seniors are safe. The routine gets automated, the complex stays human.

This smuggles in an assumption that the profession has never examined: that some work inherently contains decisions and some doesn't. Document review is "low-end." Strategy is "high-end." AI takes the empty calories, humans keep the nutrition.

But there's no such thing as decision-free work. There's only work where we've stopped noticing the decisions. Document review isn't low-end because it lacks choices. It's been *labelled* low-end because the profession decided those choices weren't worth a senior person's attention. The decisions are still there — what's relevant, what's a red flag, what pattern connects this document to that clause. A junior doing doc review with intentionality is making hundreds of micro-decisions. A senior "doing strategy" on autopilot is making none.

The hierarchy was never about the work. It was about who the profession chose to value.

And the "AI takes low, humans take high" framing makes the same mistake as Zhang's plagiarist. It looks at routine-looking output and concludes the decisions inside it were never there. Worse, it creates a false sense of safety. If you define your work as "high-end," you stop examining whether you're actually deciding or just performing seniority.

AI doesn't respect the hierarchy. It reveals that the real distinction was never junior versus senior, routine versus complex. It was always deciding versus not deciding. And that cuts across every task at every level.

Drawing lines in the sand is easy. It photographs well. It makes for a good comic. But it's a way of not deciding — of letting a blanket rule do the work that only intentionality can do.

Deciding is hard. It's slow, tiring, and expensive. It's why blanket policies exist — they're cheaper than judging case by case. It's why juniors get funnelled into production — because teaching people to decide costs more than teaching them to output. It's why the comfortable story about AI handling the low-end work is so seductive. It promises that you can skip the hard part and keep the rewards.

But I'm optimistic about this. A machine can produce a portrait with eyes closed. Only a human can choose it. And that choice can be wise or harmful, expert or naive, generous or selfish.

Here's what I believe: when we actually decide — when we stay in the work, confront what we're doing, own the choices — we are more likely to do what is right than what is wrong. Not guaranteed. More likely. The act of deciding forces a confrontation with consequences that producing never does.

That's worth protecting. Not because it's efficient. Because it's human.
